speaker: interviewer
text: It is April 2, 1995. OK, Doris.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the response was fantastic. I packed up, went to Israel, and we planned to have the first reunion to go back to Auschwitz. And as it turned out, it happened to be the 40th year to the liberation, which we felt it was very important. And we wanted to document Mengele's crimes, so we decided to have a mock trial. We were covered by the world press. In such an-- something like that, I have never seen. The cameras were shoving and pushing just to hear a word that we were saying. That did not. So after we came back from the very successful trip, and everybody was talking about, we thought that we would get the help to find our files-- to find our files. My sister was very ill. Other twins were very ill. And we felt that we could get some help now in locating those files. Just as all this happened, it fell apart very fast. Because in June, 'cause the government, the United States government, Germany, Israel, and the others, went to investigate a bunch of dry bones found in Brazil. And the reports came out that these were Mengele's bones.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, nobody even talked to-- to us. How could they find it out so fast? All are they are going to let me know 40 years later is a report on television that this is what happened? So I mortgaged my home. And I did an inquest in Terra Haute, Indiana. I felt that we, the survivors, had a right to at least question these people who were making these great conclusions.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we-- we received a copy of the forensic report. I had doctors on that-- on the panel. I had historians, psychologist. And we did the inquest. And we found out that those bones could not be Mengele's, because Mengele suffered from osteomyelitis. And the bone unearthed in Embu had no sign of osteomyelitis. But the governments of the world did not want to work on that idea. And they kept trying to prove that we were-- or we were crazy, that we were not wiling to accept. And I got in severe difficulty with all the government in the world, including Israel. In United States, after the inquest, I mailed a copy of the report to every member of the US Congress, to receive one single answer from Patrick Moynihan, who said, be careful-- very sensitive material. Then in May 6, 1986, I went to the Washington-- to Washington to participate in the capital rotunda in the Yom Ha'Shoah. And I went there to protest. I had a little poster that said, 'Memorial services are not enough. We need an open hearing on Mengelegate.' I was arrested by the capital police, which I actually call the capital gestapo. They have roughed me up to teach me a lesson. Both my arms there from here up, were blue-- extremely painful. They dragged me on the floor. My tailbone was bruised.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I read a year later a report by Elie Wiesel-- that he-- that his version of it. All the VIPs of United States were there. All the reporters were there. Not one of them did the decent thing to ask me what on earth are you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was sure that they were going to kill me. That what-- I was convinced. The way I was treated there, I had no doubt in my mind. I got out of there. Since then, I, after that, in 1988, I was a hunger strike in front of the Israeli Knesset for 13 days. All I really wanted from the Israeli government is to open the Mengele file. By then my sister was very ill. In '87 I donated my left kidney to save my sister's life. She developed cancer in 1988. And the doctors kept saying-- and I kept-- I wrote a letter to-- to President Bush. And I said, I don't know if my sister's die-- dies, I won't be able to pick up the phone Saturday and call her and find out what's going on-- how is she doing. She's the only member of my family to survive. I don't care if I ever see the files, but send the report to her doctor so they could help her-- maybe help her. Nobody, no-- no reporter, no politician, no world leader ever said, I will try to help you. I would like to hear the word, I will try to help you. Irene Hizme is suffering from severe multiple sclerosis. Peter Greenfeld, who was only 5-years-old, has a severe spinal infection that cannot be cured. There are many of these physical ailment that were strange. And nobody understands why. I will tell you that one of the saddest days of my life was when I was arrested in the capital rotunda. To be in the heart of democracy, in-- in-- with-- surrounded by the people who lead this country, and not one person even come to my rescue or to ask me what on earth is going on. I was released without any charges. And I got back to Terre Haute. Actually, when I was released, the lady who was my hostess, who was a very good friend of Elie Wiesel-- the reason I know is where we entered the capital rotunda, Elie Wiesel came up, gave her a hug and a kiss-- said to me, go back to Terra Haute. Leave the issue of Nazi criminals to us. Don't come back to Washington. Go sell real estate and be happy. This is our job. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: When I-- I-- I became so paranoid after that. When-- when I got back to Indianapolis, I was sure that my car was bombed. So I mean, how would they know where my car is? I looked in the parking lot, underneath the-- maybe there is a bomb.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got back, and I have the best boss in the world. They were big letter articles, 'Eva Kor-- Dragged, Screaming from the Rotunda.' I was afraid that I wouldn't even be able to work anymore. And my boss said, you get into some strange situations. He said, I know you better than anybody. I don't care what they are writing about you. You're doing a very good job, so you are going to stay here. In 1992, my sister developed severe-- the cancer was metastasised, and she was spitting up blood. I brought her to the United States. She was in treatment at IU Medical Center. I raised $20,000 for her treatment and put on my Visa card another $10,000. Just as I paid off the last payment, she called me from Israel in April of '93, that the cancer reappeared in her brain. I said, boy, maybe I shouldn't have paid off the last payment. Maybe then it wouldn't have happened. She died. And I decided to do something in her memory. And I said I wanted to write some kind of a book-- maybe the best way to deal with my pain. Because I had a lot of nightmares. I actually could feel the way she died. And the other thing that happened, that they wouldn't wait for me to go to the funeral. I got a telephone call message on Sunday afternoon that said, I'm sorry to tell you, but your sister died. [CRYING] I picked up the phone and called. I said, I'm packing up, getting a ticket tomorrow, and coming. No, we cannot. We have to bury her right away. I said, I never buried any member of my family. [SOB] I would like to be there, to be able to bury her. No. They wouldn't wait.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had a lot of nightmares. And I had to go to Israel to see her grave-- and to make peace with them. And I said that I need to do something in her memory. And at the same time, in 1993, I was working on a conference in biomedical ethics. And the professor said, can't you bring a Nazi doctor to the conference? And I said, what? Do you think I have in my pocket addresses of Nazi doctors?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, think about it. So I thought about seeing a doctor in one of the videos. And I contacted the people who produced it. And so I got a hold of Dr. Munch who worked in Auschwitz as a SS doctor. And I wanted to find out some information about Mengele, about the experiments, whatever he could tell me. And at the time, I invited him to come with us. I met with him. He was very receptive and very caring. I found him to be a human being, which was unusual for an SS, even now, to be human being. I said I would like him to come with us to Auschwitz to document the existence of the gas chambers, since there are so many of revisionists who are saying it didn't happen. And at the beginning, he said, no. Then he said, yes. And so we planned the trip-- with my children, his children. And when I went to Israel last year, one year to the anniversary of my-- the death of my sister--

speaker: interviewee
text: --I talked to the Israeli twins. And they said, what? An SS at our 50 year?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, well, but I want to document important information about the gas chambers. They're not on our-- on our celebration. He's going to take away some of our limelight, and we don't want to share it with him. I said, well, then tough luck. Because I am taking him anyway. So that's what we did.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I, at the time, did not know that I would want to forgive him and forgive other Nazis. But the more I thought about it, I liked him. He was willing to give me what I wanted. What do I want from a Nazi today, 50 years later? I want them to tell me that what they did was wrong, that they are sorry, and give me all the information they have. And he was willing to do all that. He said, would I be willing to forgive Joseph Mengele? Yes, I still want the information. And if he would say that he was sorry, boy, that would even be a bonus.

speaker: interviewee
text: And once I signed the paper, that amnesty, it was a very interesting feeling, because it released me from any kind of pain. Something else, for the first time-- I was in charge of my feelings towards the SS. They-- no longer did I have to struggle with the emotions. I was free of all that. And it made me feel very magnanimous, but at the same time, free-- a freedom that I have never experienced before from all the trauma of the past. It doesn't mean that I forgot. At the same time, this last year, because I wanted to write about our experiences, I wanted to dedicate my book to my sister, I thought, wouldn't it be wonderful-- I thought about a museum and a center for 10 years. I thought, in 1985, somebody would come up and write me a check for $100,000. You're doing such a great job, open a museum. Ten years later, I still didn't have anybody writing me $100,000 check. So I thought, if I am not going to do it, it will never happen. And I thought, if we could publish our own books, and the profit-- for CANDLES, as a tax-exempt organization-- publishes a book and the profit go to the center, then we could run the center. And the idea is that children-- you have to start with children. This book is dedicated to young children from age 6 to deal with hatred, to deal with prejudice, to deal with how they feel about it and what they can do about it. And I'm hoping that I can write-- take the reader with the story of little Eva and Miriam, all the way to Mengele's lab. And then we hope to write the story of all the Mengele survivors in their own words, so it would have a complete library of the survivors. Because probably, their stories maybe are not as interesting as a publisher would want to publish, but their stories have to be preserved. And we want each one of them to have a copy. And if we can make some extra money, we would like to pay them maybe 10% of the profit. So they would have-- maybe some of them do need the money. The center will be focusing on teaching the world how to deal with hatred, how to deal with prejudice. And I would love-- I would love to be able to be the survivor who will somehow start an idea as a springboard for action, that dealing with all the pains and old wounds-- it's not by holding on to them and passing it on to our children and the next generation, like some genetic disease. But it's letting go and learning from it-- to become a better person. And I think we ought to start dealing with the idea of forgiving. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] To me, I-- I would say many people think that this is strange. But they have not thought about it. They have not talked about it. And they have not tried it. And I said, until you haven't tried it, you have no idea what you are missing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Eva.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you see A dash-- very, very faint dash-- 7. I really moved my arm when they did the 0. They didn't get that one in at all. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: 0, 6, 3. See, they couldn't do to me whatever they wanted. Right? OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a liberation picture that was done in January 27, 1945. Miriam and I are in the front, next to the nurse. The nurse is carrying a young child. And there are some other young children we can see in the pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is an interesting picture, because as you could see, the liberation picture, these are the same children 40 years later at the reunion at Yad Vashem, in 1985. Miriam and I are bending down in the front of the group. The-- some of the other twins as well I can recognize. And it-- it is an interesting thing, because this is what I was hoping to do when I was looking for the Mengele twins.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This Is a picture that I found when I got back from Auschwitz, crumbled on the one-- one of the bedrooms on the floor. And this is the last picture of the family. It was taken in fall of 1943. If one looks closely, you can see apples in the trees. My father is in the middle. The-- Aliz is to the left. Edith is with her hand over her eyes, because the sun was getting in her eyes. To the-- to-- to the right of her is my friend, Luchi; my mother, Miriam-- we see-- we have both twin dolls. Miriam is to the right, and I am to the left. The cousin, Cousin Smileu, who was a-- my mother's brother's son, he is up front. And everybody in this picture is dead except me.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Trickier than she is. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's always happier, and I'm always up to something. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture that I think we were 1-year-old. And my mother told us about it when we were little. And she said we used to look like two dolls. She would put us in the window and people would walk by and they say, oh, are they real or are they dolls? And she was very proud of that. And I really do not know which one is which. And she had to put a ribbon in one of the hands to be able to kind of recognize. But our characters were different. We dressed up to-- this is a picture in Israel. I think we finally got some money and bought some civilian clothes. We were about 19 years old. And we were cruising in Tel Aviv, walking. And so we took some pictures. And this came out pretty good, so we kept it. [LAUGHS] As you can see one here. This was a picture that we have taken for high school, in Romania. We had to wear dark clothes with a beret hat. And we have sent a copy to my uncle in Israel. Now these were two individual pictures, but we put them together just-- I am on the right side, and Miriam is on the left. And the way I know is my face is a little bell-- a little bit heavier, rounder. The year this is-- this is an other side of the liberation pictures. There's-- we were marching on January 27th or January 28th between the barbed wires. The reason that it's significant-- we can see the ages of some of the children. The little boy in the middle being carried is one of the Malek twins. And I see there two little girls with white hoods on each side being carried. I do not know who they are, because I can't see their faces. But some of the children who were liberated were as young as 1 and 1/2 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture came from the Auschwitz museum. It was taken by the SS. And it's a very-- reminds me of how we felt when we stepped down from the cattle car into the selection platform. You can see a lot of people completely bewildered, not knowing what direction to go. And I can very well remember the feeling that I had when we stepped down.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture that I clearly remember. This is the way that we used to be sitting for hours. As you can see, we are painted with something. There is some kind of a dark paint that was smeared on our body. This is a way we sat for six to eight hours. And I think that this one is me, and this one is Miriam, right here. These were-- well, you can see we are all skeletons. I do not know-- I would love to see what are the notes taken during these studies. But it was extremely dehumanizing.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is a very important picture to me. I took my children to Auschwitz, January 27, 1995. This is in Birkenau, at the ruins of the crematorium. My son is standing right by me. I am wearing a t-shirt that has Miriam's picture on it. And it says, Miriam died, June 6, 1993. Because I felt that was the only way I could have her with me. My daughter is in a purple coat and standing by my son. And they are signing as witnesses to two documents that were signed. One was signed by Dr. Munch, documenting the existence of the gas chambers at Auschwitz. And the other one is mine, that I was giving amnesty to all the Nazi criminals, to all the politicians, to everybody who had something to do with covering of the cover up. And I found a great release of pain and a freedom that I have never experienced before.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is again, in January 27, 1995, the existing crematorium in Auschwitz I. We went there with a group, with Dr. Munch, the Nazi doctor, and his family. And I found it very challenging. I did not know how it was going to work out. But I was having difficulty in asking the SS doctor to light a candle. I thought, well, that was too demanding from an SS to-- to light a candle to the memory of all those died.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I asked his daughter. And she lit a candle, and she said, to the memory of all the people, you know, some people who were murdered. And then there were other. And then finally, Dr. Munch said, how come I am left out? I would like to light a candle to all the people who died here. And I even witnessed their dead-- death.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was such an emotion-filled experience, that most of the people walked away from there saying-- thanking me, that they have never experienced anything so healing and so spiritual before. And so in the next picture, we can see Dr. Munch. We are standing as one of the members of our group with Dr. Munch. And we are reciting the Kaddish. And there are the ovens in the background. And I think it was such a silent moment for everybody. It was really-- because everybody knew that this was the Jewish solemn prayer for the dead. And here was a former SS doctor standing with us and honoring the memory of those who died.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is 1985, when we went back for the 40 years to the liberation. I always felt that the selection platform was a focus of a very important of my feelings. It is a little strip of land for-- of about 40 by 150 at its longest and its widest point. And in that little strip of land, more human beings were torn-- more families were torn apart than any other place on the face of this earth. And so we left a plaque there to the memory and our pledge that we would look for the truth. And we lit six candles to the memory. And there is a wreath, a silk wreath, with silk flowers and dry leaves.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a very interesting picture. In 1991, I was contacted by ZDF, the German television, to do a documentary on the Mengele twins. And I said, what? German television?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I'm sorry. I don't want to talk to German television. I think you're very nice, but I don't want to talk to you. So he kept insisting-- called me back a week later, and said, well, listen. If you, the survivors are not going to tell us your story to the German people, who on earth is going to tell it to them? I said, OK. So as part of the documentary, they-- they took my sister, my late sister, Miriam, and me. That was December, 1991. It was bitter cold. And we went to the exactly the same place that the liberation pictures were shot.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went with the museum people. And they took a picture of us. The other thing that I want to point out, so you can see I am dressed with hat. I said, I am not going to Auschwitz to appear in a German documentary without looking as elegant as I possibly can. And so this is one of the best pictures that my sister and I have together in Auschwitz. It was very well done. They have done a very good documentary on it as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all for that group.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And then we have-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: I am waiting. I'm waiting.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was one of the project that my late sister, Miriam, worked on. She felt that at Yad Vashem, there should be something to describe our tragedy. And we collected money-- actually, this money was paid all by the Mengele twins. There, they have-- however, the committee who chose, and the place that it was placed, was by Yad Vashem. We just paid for it. As the-- this is a sculpture that shows an egg split in two, which symbolizes twins are one egg split in two. One part is made in bronze. One 1/2 is whole. That was the twin who is healthy. And the other side is chopped up and cut up, who is damaged by the experiment.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is at the dedication ceremonies. I-- by the way, my speech at the dedication ceremony was, we came a long way from Mengele's lab. And I went through all the steps that we had to take. And we each one standing by-- I stand by the side, that healthy of the egg-- by the 1/2 that's healthy. And my late sister stands by the side that is damaged by the experiments.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the document signed by Dr. Munch, at my request, documenting the existence and the way the gas chambers operated. It was my effort to be able to combat any kind of a revisionist attack on the gas chambers' existence.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was signed January 27, 1995, 50 years to the liberation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah, I think it's OK. 'Auschwitz 50 Years Later.' Yeah, it is. This is my declaration of amnesty and forgiving all the Nazi criminals, all the people who have harmed me. And I am at peace with myself. I felt that I thought somehow if-- if here from Auschwitz, I could send out a message of hope, a message of peace, a message of forgiveness-- and somehow humanity would learn to cope with all the pain, and let go of the pain-- it would be a great step toward maybe changing the way people deal with the past. Maybe children should not be-- become victims by what their parents have done or what happened to their parents. And that would be a great legacy of Auschwitz and the Holocaust, if we could do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let me see what I can take with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: You can take--
